sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 21, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The board has a gap (hollow square) at rows 24-27, cols 41-44, and two N (green cross) objects below at rows 30-33 and 36-39. The G frame selects objects. Clicking selects, directional keys likely move the selected object.
- Goal model: Move the green cross objects to fill the gap position.
- Recent findings: Current state has no G frame objects. All 5 green crosses remain unchanged. The clicks may have been just selecting without any lasting effect.
- Open questions: What's the actual goal? Need to try combining selection with other actions (directional keys or ACTION7).
- Plan: Select the top N object and move it UP into the gap, then move the bottom N up.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 20 of about 32 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
ACTION7 is not a valid action (the error says "Unknown action at index 1: 'ACTION7'"). Let me look at the valid actions again: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7. But ACTION7 failed. Maybe the valid actions list is wrong or ACTION7 needs different syntax.
Let me re-examine the situation. The UP action didn't move the N object. Let me look at what changed between steps more carefully.
Looking at the images:
- Step 17: G frame on top N
- Step 18: G frame on bottom N (this happened after a DOWN action presumably)
- Step 19: G frame back on top N (after UP)
- Step 20: Same as step 19 (after another UP that didn't change anything)
So it seems like the directional keys move the G frame (selection cursor) between the N objects, not the N objects themselves. The G frame jumped from bottom to top when I pressed UP.
Wait, but step 19 and 20 look the same. The UP at step 19->20 didn't move anything. Let me check the step numbers more carefully.
Actually, looking at the history:
- The UP action was executed at step 20 (the last action)
- Step 19 shows G on top N
- Step 20 shows G on top N (same)
So the UP action at step 19->20 didn't change the G frame position either. But earlier, going from step 17 to step 18, the G frame moved from top to bottom. What action caused that?
Let me look at the transitions more carefully. I need to understand what action caused the G frame to move between step 17 and step 18.
Actually, I think the issue is that the G frame might only move when there's an adjacent N object to move to. Since the G is already on the top N, pressing UP doesn't move it further.
Let me think about what the goal might be. The gap is at rows 24-27, cols 41-44. The N objects are below. Maybe I need to:
1. Select an N object (G frame around it)
2. Move it into the gap using directional keys
3. The N object itself moves, not just the G frame
But the UP action didn't move the N. Maybe I need to click on the N first to "activate" it, then use directional keys. Or maybe the movement is in a different direction.
Let me try clicking on the top N object (the one with the G frame) and then try moving it. Or maybe I should try RIGHT or LEFT to see if those work differently.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the board layout. The gap is at rows 24-27, cols 41-44. The N objects are at rows 30-33 and 36-39, cols 42-43. So the gap is directly above the N objects. 
Maybe the game is about moving the N objects UP into the gap. But UP didn't work. Let me try clicking on the N object itself (not the G frame) to see if that changes something.
Actually, I just realized - the ACTION7 error says "Unknown action at index 1". This might mean the action list has issues. Let me try just MOUSE actions.
Let me try clicking on the N object center to see if that does something different.
Looking at the segmentation, the top N (id=30) has boundary `[[30, 42], [30, 43], [31, 44]]`. The center would be around row 31, col 43.
Let me try clicking there.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: ACTION7 failed. Let me re-examine what's happening. The UP action seems to only move the G selection frame between N objects, not move the N objects themselves. Let me try clicking directly on the N object to see if that activates movement.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>

# Check current state and try clicking on the N object center
# Top N object center is around row 31, col 43
# Let me first check the exact position of the G frame and N

nodes = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] in ['N', 'G', 'g']:
        print(f"color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}, children={n.get('children', [])}")

# Try clicking on the center of the top N object
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 31, 'col': 43}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
